elements.sent-2411-001: We consider a run-and-tumble particle on a half-line with an absorbing target at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-002: The particle has an internal velocity state that switches between two fixed values at Poisson-distributed times.
elements.sent-2411-003: The particles evolves according to an overdamped Langevin dynamics, with a periodic force field, such that every in a given period interval are accessible to the particle.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Laplace transform of the backward Fokker--Planck equation satisfied by the survival probability of the particle yields systems of equation satisfied by the moments of the first-passage time of the particle at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-005: The mean first passage time has already been calculated assuming the particle reaches the origin in finite time almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-006: We calculate the probability that the particle reaches the origin at finite time, given its initial position and velocity.
elements.sent-2411-007: We obtain an integral condition on the force, under which the exit probability is less than one.
elements.sent-2411-008: If the force field is a constant drift, the condition is satisfied by positive values of the drift.
elements.sent-2411-009: The mean first-passage time of the particle (averaged over trajectories that reach the origin) is obtained in closed form as a consequence in both regimes.
elements.section-2411-001: Introduction, model and summary of results
elements.sent-2411-010: The mean first-passage time of a particle at a target is of interest in a variety of contexts, including search behavior or execution of limit orders in finance.
elements.sent-2411-011: They have been extensively studied in the case of Brownian particles (see \cite{redner2001guide for a review).}
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, the first-passage problem has been an active research problem in the literature; see also \cite{patie2004some, kyprianou2006introductory, duhalde2014hitting, foucart2024continuous .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The run-and-tumble particle (or RTP) has been introduced more recently as a model of active particles such as bacteria \cite{berg1972chemotaxis,berg2008coli,ramaswamy2010mechanics,cates2015motility .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this model, a particle runs in a certain direction for a certain amount of time, followed by a tumble event at which the particle takes a random velocity.
elements.sent-2411-015: A typical model to describe the motion of a particle in the one-dimensional space is the following overdamped Langevin equation: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:basic+F}\frac{dx_t}{dt}=F(x_t)+v\sigma(t),\end{equation} where $x_t$ is the location of the particle at time $t$, $F(x):\mathbb{R}\rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the external force, $v>0$ is the velocity and $\sigma(t)$ a stochastic process switching between two states $\sigma = \pm 1$ with rate $\lambda>0$.
elements.sent-2411-016: We will refer to $v\sigma(t)$ as the {internal velocity state of the particle at time $t$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The total velocity of the particle at time $t$ is the sum of the internal velocity state and the value of $F$ at the position $x_t$ of the particle.
elements.sent-2411-018: The propagator of the free run-and-tumble particle on the real line has been computed exactly \cite{othmer1988models,martens2012probability,weiss2002some (for developments in higher dimension, see \cite{Santra2020position ).}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The survival probability of a free RTP on a half line with an absorbing point at the origin, as well the non-crossing probability of two free RTPs in dimension one, have been worked out in \cite{le2019noncrossing .}
elements.sent-2411-020: If the run-and-tumble particle is subjected to stochastic resetting of its position and possibly of its velocity state (at Poisson-distributed times), the system reaches a steady state that has been computed exactly \cite{evans2018run (see \cite{Santra2020 for results in dimension two).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, models of RTPs in dimension one in the presence of force fields and boundaries have been studied \cite{malakar2018steady,le2020velocity,singh2022extremal,bressloff2023encounter,gueneau2024run, gueneau2024relating, nath2024survival, bressloff2023encounter , and some observables can be computed exactly.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper, we will restrict ourselves to the case where there is only one absorbing boundary at $0$, and $F$ is periodic.
elements.sent-2411-023: Given the initial position of the particle on the positive half-line, we will calculate the exit probability (the probability of eventually reaching the origin, which is the complementary event to survival), and the mean exit time conditioned on exit.
elements.sent-2411-024: If there are two absorbing boundaries $a, b$ with $a<b$, and a particle starts within $[a,b]$, then in \cite{gueneau2024relating the hitting probabilities for $a$ and for $b$ have been explicitly computed for general $F$.}
elements.sent-2411-025: We will use this result and an argument of Markov chain to find the exit probability rigorously and explicitly in our case.
elements.sent-2411-026: The mean exit time has been computed explicitly in \cite{gueneau2024run for $F$ of some special forms which ensure almost sure exit for the particle.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We will calculate the exit time averaged over trajectories that eventually reach the origin, for a periodic force field $F$.
elements.sent-2411-028: For a model with a periodic piecewise-linear potential, see \cite{roberts2023run .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Run-and-tumble particles with periodic force fields were studied in \cite{le2020velocity , where the emphasis was on the distribution of the position (and the mean first-passage time at a given level was obtained assuming reflecting boundary conditions at the end of a half-line).}
elements.sent-2411-030: More precisely, we consider a run-and-tumble particle in one dimension, characterized by Eq. \eqref{eqn:basic+F , with velocity $v_0$ and tumbling rate $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: The positive half-line is endowed with a periodic force field $F$ (with period denoted by $a$), hence for any $x\geq 0$: \begin{equation}F( x+a) = F(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: We want the particle to be able to reach any point in the positive half-line.
elements.sent-2411-033: The force field is therefore assumed to satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eqn:<v} |F(x)|< v.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-034: However, the above condition is not sufficient for all points within a period (including endpoints) to be accessible for a particle starting at any point in this period.
elements.sent-2411-035: We need to assume \eqref{eqn:t+ and \eqref{eqn:t- below: the equation \begin{equation}\label{eqn:t+} \frac{dx_t}{dt}=F(x_t)+v,\quad x_0=0,\end{equation} has a finite time $t_+>0$ such that $x_{t_+ =a$; and the equation \begin{equation}\label{eqn:t-} \frac{dx_t}{dt}=F(x_t)-v,\quad x_0=0,\end{equation} has a finite time $t_{- >0$ such that $x_{t_- =-a$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: To our best knowledge, conditions \eqref{eqn:t+ and \eqref{eqn:t- were not mentioned in the literature, but are necessary and sufficient to guarantee accessibility of all points within a period interval (including endpoints).}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Let us define $F$ on one period interval by $F(x)=-v+a^{-1}\gamma (a-x)^2$ for $x\in[0,a)$, assuming $a< 2v\gamma^{-1}$ for the values to bu subcritical.
elements.sent-2411-040: If the particle starts at the origin, Eq. \eqref{eqn:t+ becomes \begin{equation}\frac{dx_t}{dt}= a^{-1}\gamma (a-x_t)^2,\quad x_0=0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Solving this differential equation yields $x_t = a \gamma t(1 + \gamma t)^{-1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Hence $\lim_{t\to\infty x_t=a$, but will never reach $a$ and go beyond.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Assuming all points in a period interval including the endpoints are accessible, we will first study the exit probability, given a starting position $x>0$ and an internal velocity state: \begin{equation}E(x,\pm):= P( {\mathrm{min}}( t, x_t = 0) < \infty | x_0 = x, \sigma( 0 ) = \pm 1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: The exit probability satisfies the following system of equations: \begin{equation}\label{sysE} \begin{cases} 0 = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma E(x,- ) - \gamma E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - v) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma E(x,- ) + \gamma E(x,+ ). \end{cases}\end{equation} We will prove that the exit probability $E(x,\pm)$ is identically equal to $1$ if the force field satisfies the integral condition \begin{equation}\label{condExit} J(a)>0,\quad {\mathrm{with}} \quad J(x):= \gamma \int_0^x \frac{F( y ) }{ v^2 - F(y)^2} dy.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{sysE}  0 = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma E(x,- ) - \gamma E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - v) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma E(x,- ) + \gamma E(x,+ ). \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{condExit} J(a)>0,\quad {with} \quad J(x):= \gamma \int_0^x \frac{F( y ) }{ v^2 - F(y)^2} dy.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, we will obtain the exit probability as \begin{equation}\label{reportedE} \begin{split} E( x+Na,\pm) = \mathbbm{1}\left( J(a) \leq 0 \right) + \left[ \frac{ e^{-NJ(a)}[2\Xi_-(a) - (1-e^{-J(a)})(2\Xi_-(x)\pm e^{-J(x)}) ] }{1-e^{-J(a)} + 2\Xi_-(a)}\right] \mathbbm{1}\left( J(a) > 0 \right), {\mathrm{for}}\quad x\in [0,a[,\;\;\;\; N \in\mathbb{N},\quad {\mathrm{with}}\;\;\;\Xi_-(x):= v\gamma \int_0^x \frac{e^{-J(y)}}{v^2 - F(y)^2} dy. \end{split}\end{equation} The second quantity of interest is the mean exit time $T(x,\pm)$ of the particle that starts at coordinate $x$ with internal velocity state $\pm$, calculated over the configurations with finite exit times: \begin{equation}T( x, \pm ) = \int_0^\infty dt \, t \Phi_\pm(x,t),\end{equation} where $\Phi_\pm(x,t)$ is the flow density at time $t$ through the origin of a particle that was at position $x>0$ at time $0$.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{reportedE}  E( x+Na,\pm) = \mathbbm{1}\left( J(a) \leq 0 \right) + \left[ \frac{ e^{-NJ(a)}[2\Xi_-(a) - (1-e^{-J(a)})(2\Xi_-(x)\pm e^{-J(x)}) ] }{1-e^{-J(a)} + 2\Xi_-(a)}\right] \mathbbm{1}\left( J(a) > 0 \right), {for}\quad x\in [0,a[,\;\;\;\; N \inN,\quad {with}\;\;\;\Xi_-(x):= v\gamma \int_0^x \frac{e^{-J(y)}}{v^2 - F(y)^2} dy. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: It satisfies the system of equations \begin{equation}\label{sysT} \begin{cases} -E(x,+) = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma T(x,-) - \gamma T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - v) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma T(x,-) + \gamma T(x,+). \end{cases}\end{equation} This problem was solved in \cite{gueneau2024relating for a general force field $F$, assuming the exit probabilities $E(x,\pm)$ are identically equal to $1$.}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\label{sysT}  -E(x,+) = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma T(x,-) - \gamma T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - v) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma T(x,-) + \gamma T(x,+). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-047: In cases where the exit probability $E(x,\pm)$ is equal to 1, the quantity $T(x,\pm)$ is the mean-first passage time of the particle at the origin (given initial position and velocity state).
elements.sent-2411-048: The conditioned average over the trajectories that eventually reach the origin can be defined in all cases as \begin{equation}C(x,\pm) = \frac{T( x, \pm )}{E(x,\pm)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: These conditioned averages were calculated in \cite{de2021survival in the case of a constant force field.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In particular, if $F(x) = \mu$ for all $\geq 0$, with $\mu$ in $]-v,v[$ for all $x\geq 0$, the value of $T(0,+)$ was shown to be $(1-\mu)[\mu(1+\mu)]^{-1}$ for positive $v$, and to be $1/|\mu|$ for negative $v$.
elements.sent-2411-051: Having obtained the exit probability $E(x,\pm)$, we will solve Eq. (\ref{sysT ) and obtain $T(x,\pm)$ for a more general periodic force field.}
elements.sent-2411-052: We will show in particular that \begin{equation}\label{res2} \begin{split} T(0,+) = \left[ \frac{\Xi_+(a)}{\gamma(1- e^{J(a)})} \right] \mathbbm{1}( J(a)<0) +\left[ \frac{ e(0) \psi(a) -[ e(0) + 1] \varphi(a) }{ \gamma( 1 - e^{-J(a)})} \right] \mathbbm{1}( J(a)>0) {\mathrm{with}}\;\;\;\;E(x):= \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+) + E(x,-)\right), e(x):= \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+) - E(x,-)\right), \varphi(x):= \gamma e^{-J(x)} \int_0^x\frac{e(y)F(y) - v E(y)}{v^2-F(y)^2}e^{+J(y)} dy, \psi(x) := 2\gamma v\int_0^x \frac{\varphi(y)}{v^2-F(y)^2} dy + \gamma \int_0^x\frac{E(y) F(y) -e(y)v}{v^2-F(y)^2}dy. \end{split}\end{equation} The paper is organized as follows.

elements.sent-2411-061: Consider a run-and-tumble particle on the positive half-line, starting at position $x>0$, with a given internal velocity state $\pm v$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The particle undergoes Poisson-distributed tumble events.
elements.sent-2411-063: At each tumble event, the internal velocity state flips instantaneously.
elements.sent-2411-064: The rate of the corresponding Poisson process is called the tumbling rate and denoted by $\gamma>0$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The particle is said to be alive as long as its coordinate is positive.
elements.sent-2411-066: If the particle reaches the origin, the process stops.
elements.sent-2411-067: The time (if any) at which the particle reaches the origin, is called the exit time.
elements.sent-2411-068: Let us assume that the positive half-line $F$ is endowed with a force field satisfying \eqref{eqn:t+ and \eqref{eqn:t- , which implies \eqref{eqn:<v .}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Under this assumption, the total velocity of the run-and-tumble particle, which is the sum $F(x) + v$, is nonzero for the entire duration of the process.
elements.sent-2411-070: Moreover, the sign of the velocity of the particle $F(x_t) + v\sigma(t)$ is the same as the sign of the internal velocity state at all times.
elements.sent-2411-071: Consider the survival probability $S_\pm(x,t)$ at time $t$ of a run-and-tumble particle that was at position $x$ in the internal velocity state $\pm v$ at time $0$.
elements.sent-2411-072: This survival probability satisfies a backward Fokker--Planck equation \cite{bray2013persistence,dhar2019run , which is derived as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-073: For a particle that is alive at time $t$, and has been at position $x>0$ at time $0$ in the internal velocity state $\pm$, consider the time interval $[0, dt]$, where $dt$ is an infinitesimal time.
elements.sent-2411-074: If there is no tumble event in this interval (which is the case with probability $1-\gamma dt$), the particle is at position $x+[F(x) \pm v]dt$ at time $dt$.
elements.sent-2411-075: It must survive for a duration of time $t-dt$ to be alive at time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-076: If there is a tumble event in the interval $[0,dt]$ (which is the case with probability $\gamma dt$), the internal velocity state flips, and the particle has to survive until time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-077: Hence the two terms in the expression of the survival probability $S_\pm( x,t)$: \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = ( 1-\gamma dt )S_\pm( x + [F(x) \pm v]dt,t-dt) + \gamma dt S_\mp( x,t ).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = ( 1-\gamma dt )S_\pm( x + [F(x) \pm v]dt,t-dt) + \gamma dt S_\mp( x,t ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: Taylor expansion yields \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = S_\pm(x,t) + [F(x) \pm v]dt \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - dt \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) - \gamma dt S_\pm( x,t) + \gamma dt S_\mp( x,t),\\\end{equation} hence the evolution equation satisfied by the survival probability on the positive half-line: \begin{equation}\label{Seq} \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) = [F(x) \pm v] \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - \gamma S_\pm( x,t) + \gamma S_\mp( x,t),\quad\quad(x>0,t\geq 0).\\\end{equation} Let us denote by $\Phi_\pm(x,t)$ the density of first-passage time at the origin with a negative total velocity of a particle that started its motion at position $x$ in the internal velocity state $\pm v$.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = S_\pm(x,t) + [F(x) \pm v]dt \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - dt \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) - \gamma dt S_\pm( x,t) + \gamma dt S_\mp( x,t),\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\label{Seq} \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) = [F(x) \pm v] \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - \gamma S_\pm( x,t) + \gamma S_\mp( x,t),\quad\quad(x>0,t\geq 0).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-079: This density is related to the survival probability by the two equivalent equations \begin{equation}\label{SPhi} \begin{split} S_\pm(x,t) = 1 - \int_0^t \Phi_\pm( x, z ) dz, \Phi_\pm(x,t) = -\frac{\partial S_\pm(x, t)}{\partial t}. \end{split}\end{equation} With this definition, $\Phi_+(0,0) = 0$ because the total velocity of a particle that is at zero at time $0$ in a positive internal velocity state is $F(0)+v$ (the particle does not exit the half-line at time $0$).
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\label{SPhi}  S_\pm(x,t) = 1 - \int_0^t \Phi_\pm( x, z ) dz, \Phi_\pm(x,t) = -\frac{\partial S_\pm(x, t)}{\partial t}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: Taking the Laplace transform of Eq. (\ref{Phieq ) (assuming $e^{-st}\Phi(x,t)$ goes to zero when $t$ goes to infinity), yields the following system of equations for $\tilde{\Phi _\pm(x,s)$ (with $x>0$ \begin{equation}\label{PhiTildeEq} s\tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s) = [F(x) \pm v] \partial_x \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s) - \gamma \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s) +\gamma \tilde{\Phi}_\mp(x,s).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: We have used the fact that $\Phi_\pm(x,0)=0$ for $x>0$ (the particle does not reach the origin at time zero if it is at $x>0$).
elements.sent-2411-083: Borrowing the notations of \cite{gueneau2024relating , let us denote by $E(x,\pm)$ the probability that a particle starting its motion at time $0$ at position $x$ and velocity $\pm v$ exits the positive half-line in finite time \begin{equation}E(x,\pm) = \int_0^\infty dz \Phi_\pm(x,z) = \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,0).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-084: This exit probability appears at order $0$ in the Taylor expansion of $\tilde{\Phi _\pm(x,s)$ as \begin{equation}\label{LapExp} \begin{split} \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s):= \int_0^\infty dt \,t \Phi_\pm(x,t) + o(s) = E(x,\pm ) - s T(x,\pm) + o(s), \end{split}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}T(x,\pm):= \int_0^\infty dt ~t \Phi_\pm(x,t).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}\label{LapExp}  \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s):= \int_0^\infty dt \,t \Phi_\pm(x,t) + o(s) = E(x,\pm ) - s T(x,\pm) + o(s), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: Collecting the terms of order $0$ in $s$ in Eq. (\ref{PhiTildeEq ) yields a coupled system of evolution equations for the probability of exit in finite time, given the initial velocity state: \begin{equation}\label{sysPhiABLap} \begin{split} 0 = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma E(x,- ) - \gamma E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - v) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma E(x,- ) + \gamma E(x,+ ). \end{split}\end{equation} Collecting the terms of order $1$ in $s$ yields a coupled system of evolution equation for the mean exit times: \begin{equation}\label{sysTAB} \begin{split} -E(x,+) = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma T(x,-) - \gamma T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - v) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma T(x,-) + \gamma T(x,+). \end{split}\end{equation} These are the two systems of equations announced in the introduction in Eqs (\ref{sysE ,\ref{sysT ).}}}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\label{sysPhiABLap}  0 = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma E(x,- ) - \gamma E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - v) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma E(x,- ) + \gamma E(x,+ ). \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}\label{sysTAB}  -E(x,+) = (F(x) + v) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} +\gamma T(x,-) - \gamma T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - v) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} -\gamma T(x,-) + \gamma T(x,+). \end{equation}
elements.section-2411-003: Exit probability in a periodic force field with subcritical values
elements.sent-2411-086: For brevity, let us use $\gamma^{-1}$ as the unit of time and the mean free path $v\gamma^{-1}$ as the unit of length.
elements.sent-2411-087: The period of the force field $F$ is still denoted by $a$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The periodic force field $F$ takes only subcritical values, which in these units we can summarize as $F(x+a) = F(x)$ and $|F(x)|<1$ for all $x$.
elements.sent-2411-089: In these units, the evolution equation for the probability of exit in finite time (Eq. (\ref{sysE )) becomes \begin{equation}\label{sysExit} \begin{split} 0 = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} + E(x,- ) - E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - 1) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} - E(x,- ) + E(x,+ ). \end{split}\end{equation} Let us introduce the linear combinations, borrowing again the notations of \cite{gueneau2024optimal : \begin{equation}\label{eEDef} \begin{split} E(x) := \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+ ) + E(x,- ) \right), e(x) := \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+ ) - E(x,- ) \right). \end{split}\end{equation} In terms of these unknowns, Eq. (\ref{sysExit ) becomes \begin{equation}\label{sysHalfLine} \begin{split} 0 = F(x) E'(x) + e'(x), 0 = F(x) e'(x) + E'(x) - 2e(x). \end{split}\end{equation} Multiplying the second equation in the system by $F$ and substituting yields a differential equation of order one in $e$, \begin{equation}\label{evole} 0 = e'(x) + \frac{2F(x)}{ 1- F(x)^2 } e(x),\end{equation} which holds on the entire positive half-line because because the values of $F$ are subcritical.}}}

elements.sent-2411-115: The trajectory of the particle is continuous, but we will consider that the particle is living on the points $\{ka\}_{k\in \mathbb Z}$ in the sense that the trajectory of an RTP on the real line can be mapped to a Markov chain.
elements.sent-2411-116: If a particle is at position $ka$ in a positive velocity state, then it will either return to $ka$ with negative velocity or move to $(k+1)a$ with positive velocity, before getting out of $[ka, (k+1)a]$; if the particle is at $ka$ in a negative velocity, then it either returns to $ka$ with the positive velocity or move to $(k-1)a$ with negative velocity, before getting out of $[(k-1)a, ka]$.
elements.sent-2411-117: These alternatives allow us to introduce a Markov chain $(X_n)_{n\geq1}$ such that $X_n$ takes value either $1$ or $-1$.
elements.sent-2411-118: We define the first step of the construction at time $0$, assuming that the particle is at position $0$ (it can be either in a positive or negative velocity state).

elements.sent-2411-122: Since $F$ is periodic, $(X_n)_{n\geq 1}$ is a Markov chain.

elements.sent-2411-149: A simple periodic modification of the model with constant drift worked out in \cite{de2021survival is the one-parameter family of alternating positive and negative drifts with the same amplitude: \begin{equation}\label{alternatingDef} \begin{split} F( x + Na) = \mu I_{[0,a(1-\epsilon)]}(x) -\mu I_{]a(1-\epsilon), a[}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(x\in [0,a[,\;\;\;\;\;N\in\mathbb{N}). \end{split}\end{equation} The function $J$ is evaluated on $[0,a[$ by calculating an integral: \begin{equation}\label{JPiece} \begin{split} J(x) = \alpha x I_{[0, a( 1-\epsilon)[}(x) + \alpha( -x + 2a( 1 - \epsilon) )I_{[ a( 1-\epsilon), a]}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;\quad(x\in [0,a]), {\mathrm{with}} \;\;\;\;\;\alpha:= \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}. \end{split}\end{equation} In particular, \begin{equation}J( a ) = \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}a( 1 - 2\epsilon).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-059: \begin{equation}\label{alternatingDef}  F( x + Na) = \mu I_{[0,a(1-\epsilon)]}(x) -\mu I_{]a(1-\epsilon), a[}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(x\in [0,a[,\;\;\;\;\;N\inN). \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-060: \begin{equation}\label{JPiece}  J(x) = \alpha x I_{[0, a( 1-\epsilon)[}(x) + \alpha( -x + 2a( 1 - \epsilon) )I_{[ a( 1-\epsilon), a]}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;\quad(x\in [0,a]), {with} \;\;\;\;\;\alpha:= \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-150: The case of a constant drift corresponds to $\epsilon = 0$, and $J(a)$ is of the sign of $\mu$ if $\epsilon<1/2$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Calculating yields for $n=\pm 1$ \begin{equation}\label{XiPiece} \begin{split} \Xi_n(x) = \frac{1}{2n\mu}\left( e^{n\alpha x} - 1\right) I_{[0, a( 1-\epsilon)[}(x) +\frac{1}{2n\mu}\left( 2 e^{n\alpha a(1-\epsilon)} - 1 - e^{2n\alpha a(1-\epsilon)}e^{-n\alpha x} \right)I_{[ a( 1-\epsilon), a]}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;(0\leq x \leq a). \end{split}\end{equation} In particular, \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Xi_n(a) = \frac{1}{2n\mu}\left[ e^{n\alpha a}( 2e^{-n\alpha a \epsilon} -e^{-2n\alpha a \epsilon}) - 1\right]. \end{split}\end{equation} In particular, if $\epsilon = 0$ \begin{equation}\frac{\Xi_-(a)}{1- e^{-J(a)}} = \frac{1}{2\mu}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-160: Using the integrating factor $e^J$ we obtain \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dx}\left( e^{J(x)} t(x) \right) = -\frac{e^{J(x)}}{1-F(x)^2},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{solt} \begin{split} t(x) = t(0)e^{-J(x)} - e^{-J(x)}\int_0^x \frac{e^{J(y)}}{1-F(y)^2}dy = t(0)e^{-J(x)} - e^{-J(x)}\Xi_+(x). \end{split}\end{equation} Integrating Eq. (\ref{TPrime ) therefore yields \begin{equation}\begin{split} T(x) = T( 0 ) + \int_0^x \frac{2t(v) + F(v)}{1-F(v)^2}dv = T(0) + 2 t(0) \int_0^x \frac{e^{-J(v)}}{1-F(v)^2} dv - 2 \int_0^x \frac{e^{-J(v)}}{1-F(v)^2}\left[ \int_0^v \frac{e^{J(u) }}{1-F(u)^2}du \right] dv +\int_0^x \frac{F(v)}{1- F(v)^2} dv = T(0) + 2 t(0) \Xi_-(x) - 2 \int_0^x dv \Xi_-'(v) \Xi_+(v) dv + \frac{1}{2} J(x). \end{split}\end{equation} To fix the constants we need boundary conditions.}

elements.sent-2411-161: The particle leaves the system immediately if it starts at the origin with negative velocity: \begin{equation}\label{BCOri} T(0,-) = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-162: A second boundary condition can be obtained using the periodicity of the system.
elements.sent-2411-163: If the process starts at $a$ (at the end of a period of the force field), with positive velocity.
elements.sent-2411-164: By assumption the particle reaches the origin.
elements.sent-2411-165: To reach the origin, it must first come back to $a$ (which it does with negative velocity).
elements.sent-2411-166: Moreover, the system in which the particle lives before its first return at $a$ is a half-line equipped with a periodic force field, identical to the entire system on the half-line $[0,\infty[$.
elements.sent-2411-167: Hence the additional boundary condition \begin{equation}\label{BCPer} T(a,+) = T(0,+) + T(a,-).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: In terms of the unknowns $T$ and $t$, the boundary conditions of Eqs (\ref{BCOri ,\ref{BCPer ) imply \begin{equation}T(0) = t(0) = t(a).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Solving the equation $t(a) =t(0)$ using the expression of the function $t$ given in Eq. (\ref{solt ) yields \begin{equation}\label{t0Sure} t(0) = - \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a),\end{equation} which is the first term of the r.h.s.}
elements.sent-2411-170: This integration constant yields the expressions of the functions $t$ and $T$ as \begin{equation}\begin{split} t(x) = -e^{-J(x)}\left[ \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a) + \Xi_+(x) \right], T(x) = -\frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a)\left[ 1 + 2\Xi_-(x)\right] - 2 \int_0^x dv \Xi_-'(v) \Xi_+(v) dv + \frac{1}{2} J(x). \end{split}\end{equation} The mean first-passage times given the initial velocity state are therefore obtained as \begin{equation}\begin{split} T(x,\pm) = \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a) \left[ -1 - 2\Xi_-(x) \mp e^{-J(x)} \right] - 2 \int_0^x dv \Xi_-'(v) \Xi_+(v) dv + \frac{1}{2} J(x) \mp e^{-J(x)} \Xi_+(x). \end{split}\end{equation} Substituting the expressions obtained in the Appendix (Eqs (\ref{XiPer ,\ref{intXiPrimeXiMP ) using periodicity yields: \begin{equation}\begin{split} T(x+Na,\pm) = \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a) \left[ -1 \mp e^{-NJ(a)}e^{-J(x)} -2\frac{1- e^{-NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_-( a) -2 e^{-NJ(a)}\Xi_-(x) \right] - 2 \left[ \frac{ \Xi_+( a) \Xi_-(a)}{1 - e^{J(a)}}\left( -N + \frac{1 - e^{-NJ(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}} \right) + N \int_0^a\Xi'_-(v)\Xi_+(v) dv\right. \left.- \frac{1- e^{-NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{J(a)}}\Xi_+( a)\Xi_-(x) + \int_0^x\Xi'_-(v)\Xi_+(v) dv \right] + \frac{1}{2}\left( NJ(a) + J(x) \right) \mp e^{-NJ(a) + J(x)} \left[\frac{1- e^{NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{J(a)}}\Xi_+( a) + e^{NJ(a)}\Xi_+(x)\right], \qquad\qquad(x\in [0,a[, N\in \mathbb{N}). \end{split}\end{equation} The leading term at large $N$ in the above expression is of order $e^{-NJ(a)}$: \begin{equation}\frac{T(x+Na,\pm)}{e^{-NJ(a)}} \underset{N\gg 1}{\sim}\frac{\Xi_+(a)}{e^{J(a)} - 1} \cosh(J(x)).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-173: Substituting yields \begin{equation}\label{txExpl} \begin{split} t(x) = t(0)e^{-J(x)} + \int_0^x \frac{-E(y) + e(y) F(y)}{1-F(y)^2} e^{J(y) - J(x)} dy \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{TxExpl} \begin{split} T(x) = T(0) + 2t(0) \int_0^x \frac{e^{-J(y)}}{ 1 - F(y)^2} dy + 2 \int_0^x dz \frac{e^{-J(z)}}{1-F(z)^2}\int_0^z \frac{e^{J(y)}[-E(y) + e(y) F(y)]}{1-F(y)^2} dy +\int_0^x \frac{E(v)F(v) -e(v)}{1- F(v)^2} dv. \end{split}\end{equation} The functions $t$ and $T$ are therefore expressed in terms of the initial values $t(0)$ and $T(0)$ by \begin{equation}\label{tTExpr} \begin{split} t( x ) = t(0) e^{-J(x)} +\varphi(x), T(x ) = T(0) + 2 t(0) \int_0^x \frac{e^{-J(y)}}{1- F(y)^2}dy + \psi(x), \end{split}\end{equation} where the two functions $\varphi$ and $\psi$ do not depend on the integration constants $t(0)$ and $T(0)$.

elements.sent-2411-174: They are expressed in terms of the force field and of the functions $e$ and $E$ (worked out in Eqs (\ref{eExpr , \ref{Eexpr )) as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \varphi(x):= e^{-J(x)} \int_0^x\frac{e(y)F(y) - E(y)}{1-F(y)^2}e^{+J(y)} dy \psi(x) := 2\int_0^x \frac{\varphi(y)}{1-F(y)^2} dy + \int_0^x\frac{E(y) F(y) -e(y)}{1-F(y)^2}dy. \end{split}\end{equation} Using periodicity as in the case of almost-sure exit, we can express the functions $t$ and $T$ at position $x+Na$, with $x$ in $[0,a[$, to obtain \begin{equation}T(x+Na, \pm) = T(0) + 2t(0) \Xi_-( x+Na ) + \psi( x+Na) \pm t(0) e^{-NJ(a)} e^{-J(x)} \pm \varphi(x+Na).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-175: The calculation is shown in the Appendix.

elements.sent-2411-177: The leading term in the expression of $\psi(x+Na)$ is obtained from Eqs (\ref{FirstTermPsi ,\ref{groupTermPsi ).}}
elements.sent-2411-178: It yields the large-$N$ behavior of $T(x+Na)$ as: \begin{equation}\begin{split} T(x+Na ,\pm ) = N \left[ 2e(0)\frac{1}{1-e^{-J(a)}}\left[ \frac{\Xi_-(a)^2\Xi_+(a)}{1-e^{J(a)}}- \frac{1}{1-e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a)^2 - \Xi_-(a)\int_0^a dy\Xi'_+(y) \Xi_-(y)\right] \right]+o(N) = -\frac{1}{1 - e^{-J(a)} + 2\Xi_-(a)}\left[ \frac{\Xi_-(a)^2\Xi_+(a)}{1-e^{J(a)}}- \frac{1}{1-e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a)^2 - \Xi_-(a)\int_0^a dy\Xi'_+(y) \Xi_-(y)\right]+o(N) . \end{split}\end{equation} As $e(0)<0$, all the terms in the above equivalent are positive.
elements.sent-2411-179: Consider an initial state with positive velocity at position $a$, with a force satisfying the condition $J(a)>0$.
elements.sent-2411-180: To reach the origin in finite time, it needs to come back to position $a$.
elements.sent-2411-181: It does so with probability $E(0,+)$, because the half-line $[a,\infty[$ is endowed with the same force field as $[0,\infty[$ (because the force field is $a$-periodic).
elements.sent-2411-182: If it comes back to $a$, it passes through $a$ with negative velocity, and reaches the origin with probability $E(a,-)$.
elements.sent-2411-183: Let us denote by $C(x,\epsilon)$ the conditional expectation of the mean first passage time at the origin, for an initial state at position $x$ with velocity state $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-184: The solution of the differential equation satisfies \begin{equation}\label{DefC} T( x,\epsilon) = C( x,\epsilon) E( x, \epsilon).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-090: \begin{equation}\label{DefC} T( x,\epsilon) = C( x,\epsilon) E( x, \epsilon).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-185: The above argument implies that the total mean first-passage time of the particle starting at $a$ in the positive velocity reads \begin{equation}\label{beco} C( a,+) E( a,+) = C( 0,+) E(0,+) \times E( a,-) + E( 0,+)\times C(a,-)E( a,-) .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-091: \begin{equation}\label{beco} C( a,+) E( a,+) = C( 0,+) E(0,+) \times E( a,-) + E( 0,+)\times C(a,-)E( a,-) .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-186: This condition can be re-expressed in terms of the solutions of the evolution equation as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:T=1+1} T(a,+) = T( 0,+) E( a,-) + T(a,- ) E( 0,+),\end{equation} On the other hand, a particle leaves the system immediately if it is at the origin at time $0$ in a negative velocity state, meaning $T(0,-) = 0$, hence $T(0,+) = 1$ and \begin{equation}T(0) = t(0).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-092: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:T=1+1} T(a,+) = T( 0,+) E( a,-) + T(a,- ) E( 0,+),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-187: The quantity $t(0)$ can therefore be determined by imposing \begin{equation}T( a ) + t( a ) = 2t(0) E( a,-) + (T(a) - t(a)) E(0,+).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-190: As in the previous section, let us consider a force field that alternates between $\mu>0$ on $[0,a(1-\epsilon)[$ and $-\mu$ on $[a(1-\epsilon),a[$, with $\epsilon<\frac{1 {2}$, so that $J(a) = \mu a(1-2\epsilon)>0$.}

elements.sent-2411-192: \begin{equation}\begin{split} E(x) = E( 0 ) + \frac{e(0)}{\mu}\left( 1-e^{-\alpha x} \right) I_{[0, a( 1-\epsilon)[}(x) +\frac{e(0)}{\mu}\left( -2 e^{-\alpha a(1-\epsilon)} + 1 + e^{-2\alpha a(1-\epsilon)}e^{+\alpha x} \right)I_{[ a( 1-\epsilon), a]}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;(0\leq x \leq a). \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{phiaAlt} \begin{split} \varphi( a ) = \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1-\mu^2} \int_0^{a(1-\epsilon)} dy \left[ e(0)\mu -\left(E(0) + \frac{e(0)}{\mu}\left( 1-e^{-\alpha y} \right)\right)e^{\alpha y} \right] \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1-\mu^2} \int_{a(1-\epsilon)}^a dy \left[ e(0)\mu + \left(E(0) + \frac{e(0)}{\mu} \left( -2 e^{-\alpha a(1-\epsilon)} + 1 + e^{-2\alpha a(1-\epsilon)}e^{+\alpha y} \right)\right) e^{ \alpha( -y + 2a( 1 - \epsilon) ) }. \right] \end{split}\end{equation} The integrals are straightforward to evaluate, but the explicit form as a function of $\epsilon$ is not particularly illuminating.

elements.sent-2411-193: The value of the conditional expectation $C(0,+)$ of the exit time of a particle that leaves the system in finite time is shown on Fig. \ref{figureCondTimeAlternating .}
elements.sent-2411-194: The limits where $\epsilon$ goes to $0$ and $\epsilon$ goes to $1/2$ are interesting to work out.. $\bullet$ {\bf{Small $\epsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: To obtain a Taylor expansion of $t(0)$ at order $1$ in $\epsilon$ We have to estimate integrals of the form \begin{equation}\begin{split} \int_0^{a(1-\epsilon)} F_-(x,\epsilon)dx = \int_0^{a} F_-(x,0)dx -a\epsilon F_-(a,0) + \int_0^a \frac{\partial F_-(x,0)}{\partial \epsilon} dx \int_0^{a(1-\epsilon)} F_+(x,\epsilon)dx = \epsilon F_+(a,0) +o(\epsilon). \end{split}\end{equation} The term of order zero in $\epsilon$ in the expression of $t(0)$ is \begin{equation}\label{t0Lim} \begin{split} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim}~t(0) = \frac{1}{1-e^{-\alpha a}}\left[ -\frac{\mu}{1+\mu} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim} \psi(a) +\frac{\mu}{1+\mu} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim} \phi(a) \right] \end{split}\end{equation} The relevant limits of the integral terms are evaluated as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim} ~\varphi(x) = \frac{e^{-\alpha x}}{1-\mu^2} \int_0^a \left[ -\frac{1}{1+\mu} -\frac{\mu^2}{1+\mu} \right] dy= - \frac{1+\mu^2}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)}x e^{-\alpha x}, \end{split}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\begin{split} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim} ~\psi(a) = - \frac{2(1+\mu^2)}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)^2} \int_0^a y e^{-\alpha y }dy + \frac{2\mu}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)} \int_0^a e^{-\alpha y} dy = \frac{1+\mu^2}{(1+\mu)( 1- \mu^2)}[ y e^{-\alpha y}]_0^a - \frac{1+\mu^2}{\mu(1+\mu)( 1- \mu^2)} \int_0^a e^{-\alpha y} dy+ \frac{2\mu}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)} \int_0^a e^{-\alpha y} dy = \frac{1+\mu^2}{\mu(1+\mu)( 1- \mu^2)} a e^{-\alpha a} + \left( -\frac{1+\mu^2}{2\mu^2(1+\mu)} + \frac{1}{(1+\mu)}\right)( 1 - e^{-\alpha a}). \end{split}\end{equation} Substituting into Eq. (\ref{t0Lim ) yields \begin{equation}\label{t0Lim} \begin{split} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim}~t(0) = \frac{1-\mu}{2\mu(1+\mu)}, \end{split}\end{equation} which is the known value in the case of a constant positive drift.}
elements.formula-2411-101: \begin{equation} \int_0^{a(1-\epsilon)} F_-(x,\epsilon)dx = \int_0^{a} F_-(x,0)dx -a\epsilon F_-(a,0) + \int_0^a \frac{\partial F_-(x,0)}{\partial \epsilon} dx \int_0^{a(1-\epsilon)} F_+(x,\epsilon)dx = \epsilon F_+(a,0) +o(\epsilon). \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-104: \begin{equation} \underset{\epsilon \to 0 }{\lim} ~\psi(a) = - \frac{2(1+\mu^2)}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)^2} \int_0^a y e^{-\alpha y }dy + \frac{2\mu}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)} \int_0^a e^{-\alpha y} dy = \frac{1+\mu^2}{(1+\mu)( 1- \mu^2)}[ y e^{-\alpha y}]_0^a - \frac{1+\mu^2}{\mu(1+\mu)( 1- \mu^2)} \int_0^a e^{-\alpha y} dy+ \frac{2\mu}{(1+\mu)(1-\mu^2)} \int_0^a e^{-\alpha y} dy = \frac{1+\mu^2}{\mu(1+\mu)( 1- \mu^2)} a e^{-\alpha a} + \left( -\frac{1+\mu^2}{2\mu^2(1+\mu)} + \frac{1}{(1+\mu)}\right)( 1 - e^{-\alpha a}). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-196: In this limit, the quantity $J(a)$ is small and positive: \begin{equation}\epsilon:= \frac{1}{2} + h,\;\;\;\;\;\; h<0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-197: \begin{equation}\label{Jah} \begin{split} J(a)= \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}\int_0^{\frac{a}{2}(1-2h)}dy - \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}\int_{\frac{a}{2}(1-2h)}^a dy + o(h) = -\frac{2ah}{1-\mu^2} + o(h). \end{split}\end{equation} In this limit $J(a)$ is close to zero, and the coefficient $e(0)$ is close to $0$, its value in the cases where the particle leaves the system almost surely.. \begin{equation}e(0) = o(1),\;\;\;\;\;E(0) = 1 + o(1 ),\;\;\;\;\;E(0,+) = 1 + o(1).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-203: We have obtained a closed-form expression for the probability of exit and mean first- passage time at the origin of a run-and-tumble particle in any periodic force field whose values allow the particle to access any position on the positive real line.
elements.sent-2411-204: The particle is found to exit the system in finite time with probability $1$ if and only if the force field satisfies a condition expressed an an integral over its period.
elements.sent-2411-205: We have calculated the average of the first-passage time at the origin, over the trajectories that exit the system in finite time.

elements.sent-2411-208: In particular, the mean exit time of a particle starting at the origin in positive velocity is strictly positive, and becomes large at the separation between the two regimes of almost-sure exit and non-zero survival probability, just as in the case of a constant drift.

elements.sent-2411-273: A simple periodic modification of the model with constant drift worked out in \cite{de2021survival is the one-parameter family of alternating positive and negative drifts with the same amplitude: \begin{equation}\label{alternatingDef} \begin{split} F( x + Na) = \mu \mathbbm{1}( 0\leq x < a(1-\epsilon) -\mu \mathbbm{1}( a(1-\epsilon)\leq x < a),\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(x\in [0,a[,\;\;\;\;\;N\in\mathbb{N}). \end{split}\end{equation} An example is plotted in Fig. \ref{alternatingFig .}}
elements.formula-2411-146: \begin{equation}\label{alternatingDef}  F( x + Na) = \mu \mathbbm{1}( 0\leq x < a(1-\epsilon) -\mu \mathbbm{1}( a(1-\epsilon)\leq x < a),\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(x\in [0,a[,\;\;\;\;\;N\inN). \end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{templatePiecewise2.pdf} \caption{The field $F$ defined in Eq. (\ref{alternatingDef}), with positive $\mu$. The quantity $J(a)$ has the same sign as $\epsilon - \frac{1}{2}$.} \label{alternatingFig}
elements.sent-2411-274: The function $J$ is evaluated on $[0,a[$ by calculating an integral: \begin{equation}\label{JPiece} \begin{split} J(x) = \alpha x I_{[0, a( 1-\epsilon)[}(x) + \alpha( -x + 2a( 1 - \epsilon) )I_{[ a( 1-\epsilon), a]}(x),\;\;\;\;\;\;\quad(x\in [0,a]), {\mathrm{with}} \;\;\;\;\;\alpha:= \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}. \end{split}\end{equation} In particular, \begin{equation}J( a ) = \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}a( 1 - 2\epsilon).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-275: Substituting into Eq. (\ref{EqNapm ), the probability of exit for a particle starting at the origin in a positive internal velocity state is obtained as a function of the parameter $\epsilon$: \begin{equation}\label{exprEAlternating} \begin{split} E( 0, +) = \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2} \right) + \left[ \frac{1-\frac{1-e^{-J(a)}}{2\Xi_-(a)}}{1+\frac{1-e^{-J(a)}}{2\Xi_-(a)}} \right] \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon > \frac{1}{2} \right) = \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2} \right) + \frac{1 - \mu\left( \frac{1-e^{-\alpha a(1-2\epsilon)}}{1 - e^{-\alpha a}( 2 e^{\alpha a\epsilon} - e^{2 \alpha a \epsilon}) } \right) }{ 1 + \mu\left( \frac{1-e^{-\alpha a(1-2\epsilon)}}{1-e^{-\alpha a}( 2 e^{\alpha a\epsilon} - e^{2 \alpha a \epsilon})} \right) }\mathbbm{1}\left( 0\leq \epsilon < \frac{1}{2} \right). \end{split}\end{equation} This probability of exit is plotted in Fig. \ref{figureE0AlternatingDebug .}}
elements.sent-2411-276: One notices that for a fixed value of $\mu$, the limit where the period $a$ is small compared to $\alpha^{-1 =(1-\mu^2)/(2\mu)$ yields a function of $\epsilon$ only: \begin{equation}\label{effectiveDrift} E(0,+)\underset{a\ll 1}{\sim} \frac{1-\mu( 1 - 2\epsilon)}{1+\mu( 1 + 2\epsilon)} \mathbbm{1}( \epsilon<\frac{1}{2}) + \mathbbm{1}( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2}) .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-277: On the other hand, in the limit where the period is long compared to $\alpha^{-1}$, the probability of exit goes to $(1-\mu)/(1+\mu)$ at fixed $\epsilon$, which is the value obtained in the case of a constant positive subcritical drift (see Eq. \eqref{repro for $x=0$).}
elements.sent-2411-278: This is consistent with intuition: in the limit of large $a$, the particle is unlikely to explore a region of space where the drift is negative.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1.1\textwidth,keepaspectratio]{figureE0AlternatingDebug.png} \caption{The probability of exit $E(0,+)$ for a particle starting at the origin in an internal positive velocity state, for a drift alternating between $\mu= 1/2$ on $[0,a(1-\epsilon)[$ and $-\mu$ on $[a(1-\epsilon), a[$, as a function of $\epsilon$. The star symbols are the results of numerical simulations, with the same trajectories as in Fig. (\ref{figureE0AlternatingDebug}).} \label{figureE0AlternatingDebug}

(\ref{XiPer ) yields $\Xi'_m( ka + v ) = e^{mJ(a)}\Xi'_m( v )$, on the other hand $\Xi_n( ka +v )$ has been expressed in Eq. (\ref{XiPer ).}}

elements.sent-2411-316: The particle evolves according to an overdamped Langevin dynamics with a spatially-periodic force field such that every point in a given period interval is accessible to the particle.
elements.sent-2411-317: The survival probability of the particle satisfies a backward Fokker--Planck equation, whose Laplace transform yields systems of equations for the moments of the first-passage time of the particle at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-318: The mean first-passage time has already been calculated assuming that the particle exits the system almost surely.
elements.sent-2411-319: We obtain an integral condition on the force, under which the particle has a non-zero survival probability.
elements.sent-2411-320: The mean first-passage time of the particle (averaged over trajectories that reach the origin) is obtained in closed, both in the latter case and in the case of almost-sure exit.
elements.sent-2411-321: As an application, we consider a piecewise-constant force field that alternates periodically between two opposite values.
elements.sent-2411-322: In the limit where the period is short compared to the mean free path of the particle, the exit probability and the mean first-return time at the origin coincide with the values obtained in the case of an effective constant drift, which we calculate explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-323: The conditioned average over the trajectories that eventually reach the origin can be defined in all cases as \begin{equation}C(x,\pm) := \frac{T( x, \pm )}{E(x,\pm)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-324: These conditioned averages were calculated in \cite{de2021survival in the case where $F$ consists of a constant drift.}
elements.sent-2411-325: In particular, if $F(x) = \mu$, with $\mu$ in $]-v,v[$ for all $x\geq 0$, the value of $T(0,+)$ was shown to be $(1-\mu)[\mu(1+\mu)]^{-1}$ for positive $\mu$, and to be $1/|\mu|$ for negative $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-326: In particular, we will show that the first-return time to the origin $T(0,+)$ is finite if $J(a)\neq 0$, and that in this case \begin{equation}\label{res2} \begin{split} T(0,+) = \left[ \frac{\Xi_+(a)}{\gamma(1- e^{J(a)})} \right] \mathbbm{1}( J(a)<0) +\left[ \frac{ e(0) \psi(a) -[ e(0) + 1] \varphi(a) }{ \gamma( 1 - e^{-J(a)})} \right] \mathbbm{1}( J(a)>0) {\mathrm{with}}\;\;\;\;E(x):= \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+) + E(x,-)\right), e(x):= \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+) - E(x,-)\right), \varphi(x):= \gamma e^{-J(x)} \int_0^x\frac{e(y)F(y) - v E(y)}{v^2-F(y)^2}e^{+J(y)} dy, \psi(x) := 2\gamma v\int_0^x \frac{\varphi(y)}{v^2-F(y)^2} dy + \gamma \int_0^x\frac{E(y) F(y) -e(y)v}{v^2-F(y)^2}dy. \end{split}\end{equation} As an application, the mean first-passage time is calculated in the case where $F$ is piecewise constant and alternates between the subcritical values $+\mu$ and $-\mu$.

elements.sent-2411-335: We just need to show that if $J(a)>0$ and the particle starts at the origin with a positive velocity, then the particle stays forever in the positive half-line with a strictly positive probability.
elements.sent-2411-336: The trajectory of the particle is continuous, but we will map the trajectory of an RTP on a real line to a Markov chain (as if the particle the particle lived on the points $\{ka\}_{k\in \mathbb Z}$).
elements.sent-2411-337: If a particle is at position $ka$ in a positive velocity state, then it will either return to $ka$ with negative velocity or move to $(k+1)a$ with positive velocity, before getting out of the interval $[ka, (k+1)a]$; if the particle is at $ka$ in a negative velocity, then it either returns to $ka$ with the positive velocity or move to $(k-1)a$ with negative velocity, before getting out of the interval $[(k-1)a, ka]$.
elements.sent-2411-339: We define the first step at time $0$, assuming that the particle is at position $0$ (it can be either in a positive or negative velocity state).
elements.sent-2411-340: The other steps proceed similarly: at step $n$ we define $X_n=1$ (resp.\ $X_n=-1$) the particle has a positive (resp.\ negative) velocity.
elements.sent-2411-341: By this correspondence between the trajectory (living on the real line) and the Markov chain (living on $\{ka\}_{k\in\mathbb Z}$), we can express the following event in terms of the Markov chain: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:equivalent}\begin{split} \{\text{The particle stays forever in the positive half line starting from zero with positive velocity}\} = \left\{\forall k\geq 1 , \sum_{i=1}^kX_i>0\,\Big|\, X_1=1\right\}.\end{split}\end{equation} We just need to show that \begin{equation}\label{eqn:forallk} P\left(\forall k\geq 1 , \sum_{i=1}^kX_i>0\,\Big|\, X_1=1\right)>0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-343: The transition matrix of the Markov chain is fully determined by $p_{i,j}$ for all $i,j=1,-1$, with the following notations: \begin{equation}p_{1,1}:=\mathbb P(X_{n+1}=1\,|\, X_n=1), \quad p_{1,-1}:=1-p_{1,1}=\mathbb P(X_{n+1}=-1\,|\, X_n=1),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}p_{-1,1}:=\mathbb P(X_{n+1}=1\,|\, X_n=-1), \quad p_{-1,-1}:=1-p_{-1,1}=\mathbb P(X_{n+1}=-1\,|\, X_n=-1).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-351: In this case, $J(a)>0$ and \begin{equation}\label{explExitConst} \begin{split} \frac{2\Xi_-(a)}{1 - e^{-J(a)}} = \frac{1}{\mu},\;\;\;\;\;\;e(0) = -\frac{\mu}{1+\mu},~\;\;\;\;\;\; E(0)=\frac{1}{1+\mu}, e(x) = -\frac{\mu}{1+\mu} e^{-\frac{2\mu x}{1-\mu^2}}, E(x) = \frac{1}{1+\mu} - 2\frac{\mu}{1+\mu}\Xi_-(x) = \frac{1}{1+\mu}e^{-\frac{2\mu x}{1-\mu^2}}. \end{split}\end{equation} Hence \begin{equation}\label{repro} \begin{split} E(x,+) = \frac{1-\mu}{1+\mu} e^{-\frac{2\mu x}{1-\mu^2}}, E(x,-) = e^{-\frac{2\mu x}{1-\mu^2}}. \end{split}\end{equation} This reproduces a result in \cite{de2021survival (Eqs (45a) and (45b) there), obtained from the long-time limit of the survival probability on an RTP on the positive half-line with a constant positive subcritical drift.}

(\ref{defJ ) is evaluated on $[0,a[$ as \begin{equation}\label{JPiece} \begin{split} J(x) = \alpha x \mathbbm{1}( x\in[0, a( 1-\epsilon)[) + \alpha( -x + 2a( 1 - \epsilon) ) \mathbbm{1}( x\in [ a( 1-\epsilon), a]),\;\;\;\;\;\;\quad(x\in [0,a]), {\mathrm{with}} \;\;\;\;\;\alpha:= \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}. \end{split}\end{equation} In particular, \begin{equation}\label{JaVal} J( a ) = \frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2}a( 1 - 2\epsilon).\end{equation}.}

(\ref{EqNapm ), the exit probability for a particle starting at the origin in a positive internal velocity state is obtained as a function of the parameter $\epsilon$: \begin{equation}\label{exprEAlternating} \begin{split} E( 0, +) = \left[ \frac{1-\frac{1-e^{-J(a)}}{2\Xi_-(a)}}{1+\frac{1-e^{-J(a)}}{2\Xi_-(a)}} \right] \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon < \frac{1}{2} \right) + \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{1 - \mu\left( \frac{1-e^{-\alpha a(1-2\epsilon)}}{1 - e^{-\alpha a}( 2 e^{\alpha a\epsilon} - e^{2 \alpha a \epsilon}) } \right) }{ 1 + \mu\left( \frac{1-e^{-\alpha a(1-2\epsilon)}}{1-e^{-\alpha a}( 2 e^{\alpha a\epsilon} - e^{2 \alpha a \epsilon})} \right) }\mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon < \frac{1}{2} \right) + \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2} \right) . \end{split}\end{equation} This exit probability is plotted in Fig. \ref{figureE0AlternatingDebug .}}
elements.formula-2411-162: \begin{equation}\label{exprEAlternating}  E( 0, +) = \left[ \frac{1-\frac{1-e^{-J(a)}}{2\Xi_-(a)}}{1+\frac{1-e^{-J(a)}}{2\Xi_-(a)}} \right] \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon < \frac{1}{2} \right) + \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{1 - \mu\left( \frac{1-e^{-\alpha a(1-2\epsilon)}}{1 - e^{-\alpha a}( 2 e^{\alpha a\epsilon} - e^{2 \alpha a \epsilon}) } \right) }{ 1 + \mu\left( \frac{1-e^{-\alpha a(1-2\epsilon)}}{1-e^{-\alpha a}( 2 e^{\alpha a\epsilon} - e^{2 \alpha a \epsilon})} \right) }\mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon < \frac{1}{2} \right) + \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2} \right) . \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-358: One notices that for a fixed value of $\mu$, the limit where the period $a$ is small compared to $\alpha^{-1 =(1-\mu^2)/(2\mu)$ yields a function of $\epsilon$ only: \begin{equation}\label{effectiveDrift} E(0,+)\underset{a\ll 1}{\sim} \frac{1-\mu( 1 - 2\epsilon)}{1+\mu( 1 + 2\epsilon)} \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon<\frac{1}{2}\right) + \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-359: Comparing with Eq. (\ref{repro ), we recognize the value of $E(0,+)$ of exit in an effective constant drift $\mu( 1-2\epsilon)$.}
elements.sent-2411-360: On the other hand, in the limit where the period is large compared to $\alpha^{-1}$, the exit probability goes to the value of $E(0,+)$ obtained in the case of a constant positive subcritical drift equal to $\mu$: \begin{equation}E(0,+)\underset{a\gg \alpha^{-1}}{\sim} \frac{1-\mu}{1+\mu} \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon<\frac{1}{2}\right) + \mathbbm{1}\left( \epsilon \geq \frac{1}{2}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-361: This is consistent with intuition: in the limit of large $a$, the particle is unlikely to explore a region of space where the drift is different from $\mu$.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=1.1\textwidth,keepaspectratio]{figureE0AlternatingDebug.png} \caption{The exit probability $E(0,+)$ for a particle starting at the origin in an internal positive velocity state, for a drift alternating between $\mu= 1/2$ on $[0,a(1-\epsilon)[$ and $-\mu$ on $[a(1-\epsilon), a[$, as a function of $\epsilon$. For any value of the period $a$, the limit of $E(0,+)$ when $\epsilon$ goes to zero is $(1-\mu)/(1+\mu) = 1/3$. The star symbols are the results of numerical simulations, with the same trajectories as in Fig. (\ref{figureE0AlternatingDebug}).} \label{figureE0AlternatingDebug}
elements.sent-2411-362: For ease of notation, let us set (as in the previous section) the unit of time to $\gamma^{-1}$ and the unit of length to $v\gamma^{-1}$.

(\ref{DefTt ) as \begin{equation}\label{Txpm} \begin{split} T(x,\pm) = \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a) \left[ -1 - 2\Xi_-(x) \mp e^{-J(x)} \right] - 2 \int_0^x dv \Xi_-'(v) \Xi_+(v) dv + \frac{1}{2} J(x) \mp e^{-J(x)} \Xi_+(x). \end{split}\end{equation} Substituting the expressions obtained in the Appendix using periodicity (Eqs (\ref{XiPer ,\ref{intXiPrimeXiMP )) yields: \begin{equation}\begin{split} T(x+Na,\pm) = \frac{e^{-J(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_+(a) \left[ -1 \mp e^{-NJ(a)}e^{-J(x)} -2\frac{1- e^{-NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_-( a) -2 e^{-NJ(a)}\Xi_-(x) \right] - 2 \left[ \frac{ \Xi_+( a) \Xi_-(a)}{1 - e^{J(a)}}\left( -N + \frac{1 - e^{-NJ(a)}}{1- e^{-J(a)}} \right) + N \int_0^a\Xi'_-(v)\Xi_+(v) dv\right. \left.- \frac{1- e^{-NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{J(a)}}\Xi_+( a)\Xi_-(x) + \int_0^x\Xi'_-(v)\Xi_+(v) dv \right] + \frac{1}{2}\left( NJ(a) + J(x) \right) \mp e^{-NJ(a) - J(x)} \left[\frac{1- e^{NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{J(a)}}\Xi_+( a) + e^{NJ(a)}\Xi_+(x)\right], \qquad\qquad(x\in [0,a[, N\in \mathbb{N}). \end{split}\end{equation} The coefficient of the term $e^{NJ(a)}$ vanishes.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-427: To express the function $t$ at $x+Na$ in the case of non-zero survival probability ($J(a)>0$), we need to evaluate the integral \begin{equation}\varphi( Na + x ) = e^{-J(x+Na)}\left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \int_{ka}^{(k+1)a} dy \frac{e(y)F(y) - E(y)}{1-F(y)^2}e^{J(y)} + \int_{Na}^{Na + x} dy \frac{e(y)F(y) - E(y)}{1-F(y)^2} e^{J(y)} \right)\end{equation} Let us assume $J(a)>0$.

elements.sent-2411-429: As $F$ is $a$-periodic, \begin{equation}\begin{split} \varphi ( Na + x ) = e^{-NJ(a) - J(x)}\left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \int_{0}^{a} dy \left( \frac{F(y)}{1- F(y)^2} - \frac{ E(0) + 2e(0)\left[ \frac{1- e^{-kJ(a)}}{1 - e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_-( a) + e^{-kJ(a)}\Xi_-(y) \right]}{1-F(y)^2} e^{kJ(a) + J(y)} \right) \right. \left.+ \int_{0}^{ x} dy \left( \frac{F(y)}{1- F(y)^2} - \frac{ E(0) + 2e(0)\left[ \frac{1- e^{-NJ(a)}}{1 - e^{-J(a)}}\Xi_-( a) + e^{-NJ(a)}\Xi_-(y) \right]}{1-F(y)^2} e^{NJ(a) + J(y)} \right)\right] = e^{-NJ(a) - J(x)}\times N\left[ \int_0^a dy \frac{F(y)}{1-F(y)^2} +\frac{2e(0)\Xi_-(a)}{1-e^{-J(a)}} \int_0^a dy\frac{e^{J(y)}}{1-F(y)^2} -2e(0) \int_0^a dy\frac{e^{J(y)}\Xi_-(y)}{1-F(y)^2} \right] -e^{-NJ(a) - J(x)}\times \int_0^a dy \left( E(0) +\frac{2e(0)}{1-e^{-J(a)}} \right) \frac{1-e^{N J(a)}}{(1-F(y)^2)( 1 - e^{J(a)})}e^{J(y)} + e^{-NJ(a) - J(x)} \left[ \int_0^x dy \frac{F(y)}{1-F(y)^2} -\left( E(0) + 2e(0) \frac{1-e^{-NJ(a)}}{1-e^{-J(a)}} \right)e^{NJ(a)} \int_0^x dy\frac{e^{J(y)}}{1-F(y)^2}\right. \left. -2e(0) \int_0^x dy\frac{e^{J(y)}\Xi_-(y)}{1-F(y)^2} \right]. \end{split}\end{equation} Let us reorganize the above expression by grouping the terms according to their dependence on $N$.

elements.sent-2411-432: The particle has an internal velocity state that switches between two opposite values at Poisson-distributed times.
elements.sent-2411-433: The position of the particle evolves according to an overdamped Langevin dynamics with a spatially-periodic force field such that every point in a given period interval is accessible to the particle.
elements.sent-2411-434: The conditioned average of the first-passage time at the origin (over the trajectories that reach the origin) is obtained in closed form, both in the latter case and in the case of almost-sure exit.
elements.sent-2411-435: It has been extensively studied in the case of Brownian particles (see for example \cite{patie2004some, kyprianou2006introductory, duhalde2014hitting, foucart2024continuous , and \cite{redner2001guide for a review).}}
elements.sent-2411-436: A typical model to describe the motion of a particle on a straight line is the following overdamped Langevin equation: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:basic+F}\frac{dx_t}{dt}=F(x_t)+v\sigma(t),\end{equation} where $x_t$ is the position of the particle at time $t$, $F(x):\mathbb{R}\rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is the external force, $v>0$ is a fixed velocity, and $\sigma(t)$ a stochastic process switching between the values $+1$ and $-1$ with {tumbling rate $\gamma>0$ (for a formulation in terms of stochastic partial differential equations, see \cite{bressloff2024stochastic ).}}
elements.sent-2411-437: The propagator of the free run-and-tumble particle on the real line has been calculated exactly \cite{othmer1988models,martens2012probability,weiss2002some (for developments in higher dimension, see \cite{Santra2020position ).}}
elements.sent-2411-438: The survival probability of a free RTP on a half-line with an absorbing point at the origin, as well as the non-crossing probability of two free RTPs in dimension one, have been calculated in \cite{le2019noncrossing .}
elements.sent-2411-439: Moreover, models of RTPs in dimension one in the presence of force fields and boundaries have been studied \cite{malakar2018steady,le2020velocity,singh2022extremal,bressloff2022encounter,bressloff2023encounter,gueneau2024run, gueneau2024relating, nath2024survival , and some observables can be computed exactly.}
elements.sent-2411-440: If there are two absorbing boundaries $a, b$ with $a<b$, and a particle starts within $[a,b]$, the hitting probabilities for $a$ and for $b$ have been explicitly computed in \cite{gueneau2024relating for any $F$.}
elements.sent-2411-441: More precisely, we consider a run-and-tumble particle in one dimension, characterized by Eq. \eqref{eqn:basic+F , with velocity $v$ and tumbling rate $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-442: The positive half-line is endowed with a periodic force field $F$ (with period denoted by $a$): for any $x\geq 0$, \begin{equation}F( x+a) = F(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-443: However, the above condition is not sufficient for all points within a period (including endpoints) to be accessible to a particle starting at any point in this period.
elements.sent-2411-444: We need to make the following two assumptions: $(i)$ the equation \begin{equation}\label{eqn:t+} \frac{dx_t}{dt}=F(x_t)+v,\quad x_0=0,\end{equation} induces a finite time $t_+>0$ such that $x_{t_+ =a$; $(ii)$ the equation \begin{equation}\label{eqn:t-} \frac{dx_t}{dt}=F(x_t)-v,\quad x_0=0,\end{equation} induces a finite time $t_{- >0$ such that $x_{t_- =-a$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-446: To our best knowledge, conditions $(i)$ and $(ii)$ have not been mentioned in the literature, but are necessary and sufficient to guarantee accessibility of all points within a period interval (including endpoints).
elements.sent-2411-447: Let us give a counterexample for condition $(i)$.
elements.sent-2411-448: Hence $\lim_{t\to\infty x_t=a$, but the particle will never reach $a$ and go beyond.}
elements.sent-2411-449: Assuming all points in a period interval including the endpoints are accessible, we will first study the exit probability, given an initial position $x>0$ and an initial internal velocity state: \begin{equation}E(x,\pm):= P( {\mathrm{min}}( t, x_t = 0) < \infty | x_0 = x, \sigma( 0 ) = \pm 1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-450: For convenience, let us work in a system of units where the unit of length is the mean free path $v\gamma^{-1}$ and the unit of time is the mean $\gamma^{-1}$ of the Poisson-distributed times between tumble events.
elements.sent-2411-451: This is achieved by setting the parameters $v$ and $\gamma$ to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-452: The exit probability satisfies the following system of equations: \begin{equation}\label{sysE} \begin{cases} 0 = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} + E(x,- ) - E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - 1) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} - E(x,- ) + E(x,+ ). \end{cases}\end{equation} Another quantity of interest is the mean exit time $T(x,\pm)$ of the particle that starts at coordinate $x$ with internal velocity state $\pm$, calculated over the configurations with finite exit times: \begin{equation}T( x, \pm ) = \int_0^\infty dt \, t \Phi_\pm(x,t),\end{equation} where $\Phi_\pm(x,t)$ is the flow density at time $t$ through the origin of a particle that was at coordinate $x$ with internal velocity state $\pm$ at time $0$.
elements.formula-2411-206: \begin{equation}\label{sysE}  0 = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} + E(x,- ) - E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - 1) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} - E(x,- ) + E(x,+ ). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-453: The quantities $T(x,\pm)$ satisfy the system of equations \begin{equation}\label{sysT} \begin{cases} -E(x,+) = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} + T(x,-) - T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - 1) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} -T(x,-) + T(x,+). \end{cases}\end{equation} The conditioned average over the trajectories that eventually reach the origin can be defined in all cases as \begin{equation}\langle T(x)\rangle_{c,\pm} := \frac{T(x,\pm)}{E(x,\pm)},\end{equation} where $c$ in the above notation indicates that the average is conditioned on the exit of the particle.
elements.formula-2411-207: \begin{equation}\label{sysT}  -E(x,+) = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} + T(x,-) - T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - 1) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} -T(x,-) + T(x,+). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-454: In cases where the particle with a given initial velocity state exits the system almost surely, the conditioned average $\langle T(x)\rangle_{c,\pm}$ is the mean first-passage time at the origin, which coincides with $T(x,\pm)$.
elements.sent-2411-455: The exit probabilities $E(x,\pm)$ and the conditioned averages $\langle T(x,\pm)\rangle_c$ were calculated in \cite{de2021survival in the case where $F$ consists of a constant drift.}
elements.sent-2411-456: Two regimes were identified depending on the sign of the drift.
elements.sent-2411-457: The particle exits the system almost surely if the drift is negative, otherwise its exit probability exit decreases exponentially with the starting distance from the origin.
elements.sent-2411-458: The corresponding statements are as follows: {\bf{$(\mathrm{I )_{\mu}$ Exit probability with a constant drift $\mu$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-459: If $F(x)=\mu$ for all $x\geq 0$, then \begin{equation}\begin{split} E(x,+) = \mathbbm{1}( \mu \leq 0 ) + \frac{1-\mu}{1+\mu}e^{-\frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2} x}\mathbbm{1}( \mu > 0 ), E(x,-) = \mathbbm{1}( \mu \leq 0 ) + e^{-\frac{2\mu}{1-\mu^2} x}\mathbbm{1}( \mu > 0 ). \end{split}\end{equation} The mean first-passage time at the origin is infinite if the drift is equal to zero.
elements.sent-2411-460: Otherwise, the conditioned averages $\langle T(x,\pm)\rangle_c$ satisfy {\bf{$(\mathrm{II )_{\mu}$ Conditioned average of the first-passage time over trajectories that reach the origin, with a constant drift $\mu$.}}}

(\ref{sysT ) was solved in \cite{gueneau2024relating for a general force field $F$, assuming the exit probabilities $E(x,\pm)$ are identically equal to $1$.}}

elements.sent-2411-473: Consider a run-and-tumble particle on the positive half-line, starting its motion at position $x>0$, with a given internal velocity state $\pm 1$ (the unit of velocity is set to the absolute value of the internal velocity).
elements.sent-2411-474: The rate $\gamma$ of the corresponding Poisson process (the tumbling rate) is set to $1$ ($\gamma^{-1}$ is chosen as the unit of time).
elements.sent-2411-475: Let us assume that the positive half-line is endowed with a field $F$ satisfying the conditions $(i)$ and $(ii)$ of Eqs (\eqref{eqn:t+ ,\eqref{eqn:t- ), which implies \eqref{eqn:<v .}}}
elements.sent-2411-476: Under this assumption, the total velocity of the run-and-tumble particle, which is the sum $F(x_t) + \sigma(t)$ is nonzero for the duration of the process.
elements.sent-2411-477: Moreover, the sign of the total velocity of the particle is the same as the sign of the internal velocity $\sigma(t)$ at all times.
elements.sent-2411-478: Consider the survival probability $S_\pm(x,t)$ at time $t$ of a run-and-tumble particle that was at position $x$ in the internal velocity state $\pm 1$ at time $0$.
elements.sent-2411-479: For a particle that is alive at time $t$, and has been at position $x>0$ at time $0$ in the internal velocity state $\pm 1$, consider the time interval $[0, dt]$, where $dt$ is an infinitesimal time.
elements.sent-2411-480: If there is no tumble event in this interval (which is the case with probability $1- dt$), the particle is at position $x+[F(x) \pm 1]dt$ at time $dt$.
elements.sent-2411-481: If there is a tumble event in the interval $[0,dt]$ (which is the case with probability $dt$), the internal velocity state flips, and the particle has to survive until time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-482: Hence the two terms in the expression of the survival probability $S_\pm( x,t)$: \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = ( 1- dt )S_\pm( x + [F(x) \pm 1]dt,t-dt) + dt S_\mp( x,t ).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-214: \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = ( 1- dt )S_\pm( x + [F(x) \pm 1]dt,t-dt) + dt S_\mp( x,t ).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-483: Taylor expansion yields \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = S_\pm(x,t) + [F(x) \pm 1]dt \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - dt \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) - dt S_\pm( x,t) + dt S_\mp( x,t).\\\end{equation} Hence the evolution equation satisfied by the survival probability on the positive half-line: \begin{equation}\label{Seq} \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) = [F(x) \pm 1] \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - S_\pm( x,t) + S_\mp( x,t),\quad\quad(x>0,t\geq 0).\\\end{equation} Let us denote by $\Phi_\pm(x,t)$ the density of first-passage time at the origin with a negative total velocity of a particle that started its motion (at time $0$) at position $x$ with the internal velocity state $\pm v$: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Phi_\pm(x,t) = -\frac{\partial S_\pm(x, t)}{\partial t}. \end{split}\end{equation} With this definition, $\Phi_+(0,0) = 0$ because the total velocity of a particle that is at zero at time $0$ in a positive internal velocity state is $F(0)+v>0$ (the particle does not exit the half-line at time $0$).
elements.formula-2411-215: \begin{equation}S_\pm( x,t) = S_\pm(x,t) + [F(x) \pm 1]dt \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - dt \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) - dt S_\pm( x,t) + dt S_\mp( x,t).\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-216: \begin{equation}\label{Seq} \partial_t S_\pm(x,t) = [F(x) \pm 1] \partial_x S_\pm(x,t) - S_\pm( x,t) + S_\mp( x,t),\quad\quad(x>0,t\geq 0).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-484: Taking the Laplace transform of Eq. (\ref{Phieq ) (assuming $e^{-st}\Phi(x,t)$ goes to zero when $t$ goes to infinity), yields the following system of equations for $\tilde{\Phi _\pm(x,s)$ (with $x>0$): \begin{equation}\label{PhiTildeEq} s\tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s) = [F(x) \pm 1] \partial_x \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s) - \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s) + \tilde{\Phi}_\mp(x,s).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-485: Borrowing the notations of \cite{gueneau2024relating , let us denote by $E(x,\pm)$ the probability that a particle starting its motion at time $0$ at position $x$ with internal velocity state $\pm v$ exits the positive half-line in finite time \begin{equation}E(x,\pm) = \int_0^\infty dz \Phi_\pm(x,z) = \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,0).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-486: This exit probability appears at order $0$ in the Taylor expansion of $\tilde{\Phi _\pm(x,s)$ around $s=0$ as \begin{equation}\label{LapExp} \begin{split} \tilde{\Phi}_\pm(x,s):= \int_0^\infty dt \,t \Phi_\pm(x,t) + o(s) = E(x,\pm ) - s T(x,\pm) + o(s), \end{split}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}T(x,\pm):= \int_0^\infty dt ~t \Phi_\pm(x,t)\end{equation} is the mean exit time (given that the particle having started at $x$ in the velocity state $\pm$ exits the system).}
elements.sent-2411-487: Collecting the terms of order $0$ in $s$ in Eq. (\ref{PhiTildeEq ) yields a coupled system of evolution equations for the exit probability in finite time, given the initial velocity state: \begin{equation}\label{sysPhiABLap} \begin{split} 0 = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} + E(x,- ) - E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - 1) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} - E(x,- ) + E(x,+ ). \end{split}\end{equation} Collecting the terms of order $1$ in $s$ yields a coupled system of evolution equation for the mean exit times: \begin{equation}\label{sysTAB} \begin{split} -E(x,+) = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial T(x,+)}{\partial x} + T(x,-) - T(x,+), -E(x,-) = (F(x) - 1) \frac{\partial T(x,- )}{\partial x} - T(x,-) + T(x,+). \end{split}\end{equation} These are the two systems of equations (Eqs (\ref{sysE ,\ref{sysT )) announced in the introduction.}}}

(\ref{sysE )) becomes \begin{equation}\label{sysExit} \begin{split} 0 = (F(x) + 1) \frac{\partial E(x,+)}{\partial x} + E(x,- ) - E(x,+ ), 0 = (F(x) - 1) \frac{ \partial E(x,- )}{\partial x} - E(x,- ) + E(x,+ ). \end{split}\end{equation} Let us introduce the linear combinations, borrowing again the notations of \cite{gueneau2024optimal : \begin{equation}\label{eEDef} \begin{split} E(x) := \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+ ) + E(x,- ) \right), e(x) := \frac{1}{2}\left( E(x,+ ) - E(x,- ) \right). \end{split}\end{equation} In terms of these unknowns, Eq. (\ref{sysExit ) becomes \begin{equation}\label{sysHalfLine} \begin{split} 0 = F(x) E'(x) + e'(x), 0 = F(x) e'(x) + E'(x) - 2e(x). \end{split}\end{equation} Multiplying the second equation in the system by $F$ and substituting yields a differential equation of order one in $e$, \begin{equation}\label{evole} 0 = e'(x) + \frac{2F(x)}{ 1- F(x)^2 } e(x).\end{equation}.}}}

elements.section-2411-008: Conditioned average of the exit time over trajectories that reach the origin

elements.subsubsection-2411-008: Almost-sure exit with finite mean first-passage at the origin ($J(a)<0$)
elements.sent-2411-523: As the particle exits the system almost surely, $E(0,+)=1$, and $\langle T(0)\rangle_{c,+}$ also becomes large.
elements.sent-2411-524: This is consistent with the vertical asymptote observed in the plot of $\langle T(0)\rangle_{c,+}$ as a function of $\epsilon$ in Fig. \ref{figureCondTimeAlternatingDebug .}
elements.sent-2411-525: As the particle exits the system almost surely, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{C0PlusAs} \langle T(0)\rangle_{c,+} \underset{a\ll 1}{\sim} \frac{1}{|\mu(1-2\epsilon)|}.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-010: Checks in the case of a constant drift
elements.sent-2411-549: In the case of a constant drift, the quantity $\alpha$ is $\frac{2\mu {1-\mu^2 <0$ for $x$ in $[0,a]$.}}
